title: Finding Mean, Median, and Mode of {3, 5, 7, 5, 10}

steps.instruction: The mean is the sum of all values divided by how many values there are.
steps.explanation: The mean (average) tells us what each value would be if the total were shared equally.

steps.instruction: Arrange the values from least to greatest.
steps.explanation: The median requires the data to be sorted first.

steps.instruction: The median is the middle value. With 5 values, the middle is the 3rd value.
steps.explanation: With an odd number of values, the median is the single middle value.

steps.instruction: The mode is the value that appears most often.
steps.work: 3 appears 1 time. 5 appears 2 times. 7 appears 1 time. 10 appears 1 time. Mode = 5.
steps.explanation: The number 5 appears more than any other value, making it the mode.
These three measures each describe the center of the data in a different way.

title: Finding Mean, Median, and Mode of {12, 15, 18, 20, 15, 22}

steps.instruction: Add all the values and divide by the count.
steps.explanation: The total of all six values is 102, and dividing equally among 6 gives 17.

steps.instruction: Arrange the values from least to greatest.
steps.explanation: Sorting is required before finding the median.

steps.instruction: With 6 values (even count), the median is the average of the 3rd and 4th values.
steps.explanation: With an even number of values, there is no single middle value, so we average the two middle values.

steps.instruction: Identify which value appears most frequently.
steps.work: 12: once. 15: twice. 18: once. 20: once. 22: once. Mode = 15.
steps.explanation: 15 appears twice while every other value appears only once, so 15 is the mode.
Notice that mean, median, and mode can all be different values for the same data set.
